community

Find Your Local Beekeeping Club or Association

Find local beekeeping clubs, state associations, and national organizations near you. Connect with mentors, attend meetings, and join the beekeeping community.

Whether you're a complete beginner or an experienced beekeeper, joining a local club is the fastest way to learn, troubleshoot problems, and connect with mentors in your area. This directory lists beekeeping associations and clubs across all 50 states, plus national organizations that serve the entire beekeeping community.

Why Every Beekeeper Should Join a Local Club

Joining a beekeeping club is one of the smartest decisions you can make, whether you're setting up your first hive or managing a dozen. Here's why experienced beekeepers consistently recommend it:

  • Hands-on learning from experienced beekeepers: You get real-world advice that no book can provide. Club members have dealt with the exact pests, diseases, and weather patterns in your region.
  • Avoid costly mistakes: Beginners who skip club membership often spend hundreds on unnecessary equipment or make management errors that damage their colonies. Club mentors help you avoid these pitfalls.
  • Group equipment purchases: Clubs negotiate bulk discounts on hives, frames, and protective gear. You'll save 15-25% compared to retail prices.
  • Shared equipment access: Many clubs own extractors, uncapping machines, and queen rearing equipment members can borrow. This saves thousands in personal investment.
  • Swarm call lists: Club members get first access to free or low-cost swarms—the cheapest way to start new colonies.
  • Local knowledge: You'll learn which nectar flows happen when, which treatments work best for local pests, and how to prepare for your region's specific challenges.
  • Mentorship programs: Most clubs formally pair beginners with experienced beekeepers who check in regularly during your first season.
  • Social connection: Beekeeping can feel isolating. Clubs give you a community of people who genuinely understand why you're excited about bee behavior and honey production.

National Beekeeping Organizations

These umbrella organizations support state and local clubs, advocate for beekeeping interests, and provide resources for the entire beekeeping community:

American Beekeeping Federation (ABF)

The largest national organization representing beekeepers and the beekeeping industry. ABF advocates for beekeeper interests at the federal level, publishes the American Bee Journal, and hosts the annual American Beekeeping Federation Convention. Membership includes access to legislative updates, networking opportunities, and discounts on beekeeping supplies. Website: abfnet.org

Apiary Inspectors of America (AIA)

Professional organization for state and federal apiary inspectors and bee scientists. While primarily for inspectors, AIA resources help beekeepers understand disease management and regulatory standards. They publish guidelines on bee health and pest identification. Website: apiaryinspectors.org

American Honey Producers Association (AHPA)

Focused on commercial and sideline beekeepers interested in honey production. AHPA advocates for fair honey pricing, participates in trade negotiations, and provides market information. Membership is particularly valuable if you plan to sell honey or bee products. Website: americanhoneyproducers.org

Project Apis m. (PAm)

Research-focused organization dedicated to improving bee health through scientific study. PAm funds research on bee diseases, pesticide impacts, and nutrition. Supporting PAm helps fund critical research that benefits all beekeepers. Website: projectapism.org

The Bee Informed Partnership

Academic research consortium tracking bee colony health across North America. Beekeepers can participate in annual surveys that contribute to the largest dataset on colony loss and management practices. Website: beeinformed.org

State Beekeeping Associations

Every state has a primary beekeeping association that coordinates local clubs, hosts state conferences, and advocates for beekeeping interests. Use this list to find your state's main organization:

Alabama

Alabama Beekeepers Association — Hosts annual state conference, publishes monthly newsletter, maintains list of local clubs. Website: alabamabeekeepers.org

Alaska

Alaska Beekeepers Association — Supports beekeeping in challenging northern climate, offers resources on winter preparation and short-season management. Website: alaskabeekeepers.org

Arizona

Arizona Beekeepers Association — Focuses on desert beekeeping practices, coordinates swarm removal network, hosts spring and fall conferences. Website: azbeekeepers.org

Arkansas

Arkansas Beekeepers Association — Operates mentorship program for beginners, maintains directory of local clubs, publishes quarterly journal. Website: arkansasbeekeepers.org

California

California State Beekeepers Association — Largest state association with 20+ local clubs. Hosts annual convention, advocates on agricultural policy, maintains research library. Website: californiabeekeepers.org

Colorado

Colorado Beekeepers Association — Coordinates 15 regional clubs, publishes monthly newsletter, hosts spring and fall conferences. Website: coloradobeekeepers.org

Connecticut

Connecticut Beekeepers Association — Operates beginner certification program, maintains swarm removal list, coordinates with state agricultural department. Website: ctbeekeepers.org

Delaware

Delaware Beekeepers Association — Small but active association, hosts monthly meetings, provides mentorship for new beekeepers. Website: delawarebeekeepers.org

Florida

Florida Beekeepers Association — Manages 30+ local clubs across the state, hosts annual conference, provides resources on tropical bee management and Africanized bee protocols. Website: floridabeekeepers.org

Georgia

Georgia Beekeepers Association — One of the oldest state associations, coordinates 40+ local clubs, publishes monthly magazine, hosts spring and fall conferences. Website: gabeekeepers.org

Hawaii

Hawaii Beekeepers Association — Supports beekeeping on islands, coordinates with local agricultural extension, provides resources on tropical pest management. Website: hawaiibeekeepers.org

Idaho

Idaho Beekeepers Association — Coordinates regional clubs, hosts annual conference, maintains research on cold-climate beekeeping. Website: idahobeekeepers.org

Illinois

Illinois State Beekeepers Association — Operates 25+ local clubs, publishes monthly bulletin, hosts spring and fall conferences. Website: illinoisbeekeepers.org

Indiana

Indiana Beekeepers Association — Coordinates regional clubs, hosts annual conference, provides mentorship program for beginners. Website: indianabeekeepers.org

Iowa

Iowa Beekeepers Association — Manages 20+ county clubs, publishes quarterly journal, hosts annual conference. Website: iowabeekeepers.org

Kansas

Kansas Beekeepers Association — Coordinates regional clubs, hosts spring and fall conferences, maintains swarm removal network. Website: kansasbeekeepers.org

Kentucky

Kentucky Beekeepers Association — Operates mentorship program, coordinates 15+ local clubs, hosts annual conference. Website: kybeekeepers.org

Louisiana

Louisiana Beekeepers Association — Supports beekeeping in subtropical climate, coordinates with state agricultural extension, hosts annual conference. Website: labeekeepers.org

Maine

Maine Beekeepers Association — Coordinates 20+ local clubs, publishes monthly newsletter, hosts spring and fall conferences focused on cold-climate management. Website: mainebeekeepers.org

Maryland

Maryland Beekeepers Association — Operates beginner certification program, coordinates 15+ local clubs, hosts annual conference. Website: mdbeekeepers.org

Massachusetts

Massachusetts Beekeepers Association — One of the oldest state associations, coordinates 30+ local clubs, publishes monthly magazine, hosts spring and fall conferences. Website: massbeekeepers.org

Michigan

Michigan Beekeepers Association — Manages 25+ regional clubs, publishes monthly bulletin, hosts annual conference. Website: michiganbeekeepers.org

Minnesota

Minnesota Beekeepers Association — Coordinates 20+ local clubs, publishes quarterly journal, hosts spring and fall conferences. Website: mnbeekeepers.org

Mississippi

Mississippi Beekeepers Association — Operates mentorship program, coordinates local clubs, hosts annual conference. Website: msbeekeepers.org

Missouri

Missouri Beekeepers Association — Manages 20+ regional clubs, publishes monthly newsletter, hosts spring and fall conferences. Website: mobeekeepers.org

Montana

Montana Beekeepers Association — Coordinates regional clubs, hosts annual conference, provides resources on mountain beekeeping. Website: montanabeekeepers.org

Nebraska

Nebraska Beekeepers Association — Operates 15+ local clubs, publishes quarterly bulletin, hosts annual conference. Website: nebraska beekeepers.org

Nevada

Nevada Beekeepers Association — Supports desert beekeeping, coordinates with state agricultural extension, hosts annual conference. Website: nevadabeekeepers.org

New Hampshire

New Hampshire Beekeepers Association — Coordinates 15+ local clubs, publishes monthly newsletter, hosts spring and fall conferences. Website: nhbeekeepers.org

New Jersey

New Jersey Beekeepers Association — Manages 20+ regional clubs, publishes monthly bulletin, hosts spring and fall conferences. Website: njbeekeepers.org

New Mexico

New Mexico Beekeepers Association — Supports desert and high-altitude beekeeping, coordinates regional clubs, hosts annual conference. Website: nmbeekeepers.org

New York

New York State Beekeepers Association — One of the largest state associations with 50+ local clubs, publishes monthly magazine, hosts spring and fall conferences. Website: nysbeekeepers.org

North Carolina

North Carolina Beekeepers Association — Coordinates 30+ regional clubs, publishes monthly newsletter, hosts spring and fall conferences. Website: ncbeekeepers.org

North Dakota

North Dakota Beekeepers Association — Manages regional clubs, hosts annual conference, provides resources on cold-climate beekeeping. Website: ndbeekeepers.org

Ohio

Ohio Beekeepers Association — Operates 25+ local clubs, publishes monthly bulletin, hosts spring and fall conferences. Website: ohiobeekeepers.org

Oklahoma

Oklahoma Beekeepers Association — Coordinates regional clubs, hosts annual conference, maintains swarm removal network. Website: okbeekeepers.org

Oregon

Oregon Beekeepers Association — Manages 20+ regional clubs, publishes quarterly journal, hosts annual conference. Website: oregonbeekeepers.org

Pennsylvania

Pennsylvania Beekeepers Association — Operates 30+ local clubs, publishes monthly magazine, hosts spring and fall conferences. Website: pabeekeepers.org

Rhode Island

Rhode Island Beekeepers Association — Small but active association, coordinates with local clubs, hosts monthly meetings. Website: ribeekeepers.org

South Carolina

South Carolina Beekeepers Association — Manages 20+ regional clubs, publishes monthly newsletter, hosts spring and fall conferences. Website: scbeekeepers.org

South Dakota

South Dakota Beekeepers Association — Coordinates regional clubs, hosts annual conference, provides resources on prairie beekeeping. Website: sdbeekeepers.org

Tennessee

Tennessee Beekeepers Association — Operates 25+ local clubs, publishes monthly bulletin, hosts spring and fall conferences. Website: tnbeekeepers.org

Texas

Texas Beekeepers Association — Largest state association with 50+ regional clubs, publishes monthly magazine, hosts spring and fall conferences. Website: texasbeekeepers.org

Utah

Utah Beekeepers Association — Coordinates regional clubs, hosts annual conference, provides resources on high-altitude beekeeping. Website: utahbeekeepers.org

Vermont

Vermont Beekeepers Association — Coordinates 15+ local clubs, publishes quarterly journal, hosts spring and fall conferences. Website: vermontbeekeepers.org

Virginia

Virginia Beekeepers Association — Manages 25+ regional clubs, publishes monthly newsletter, hosts spring and fall conferences. Website: vabeekeepers.org

Washington

Washington State Beekeepers Association — Coordinates 20+ regional clubs, publishes quarterly bulletin, hosts annual conference. Website: wasbeekeepers.org

West Virginia

West Virginia Beekeepers Association — Operates mentorship program, coordinates local clubs, hosts annual conference. Website: wvbeekeepers.org

Wisconsin

Wisconsin Beekeepers Association — Manages 25+ regional clubs, publishes monthly magazine, hosts spring and fall conferences. Website: wisconsinbeekeepers.org

Wyoming

Wyoming Beekeepers Association — Coordinates regional clubs, hosts annual conference, provides resources on high-altitude and prairie beekeeping. Website: wyomingbeekeepers.org

Local Beekeeping Clubs by State

This section provides local clubs organized by state. Most clubs meet monthly and welcome visitors before requiring membership. Contact information and meeting details are listed where available.

Alabama

  • Birmingham Beekeepers Club — Birmingham area. Meets 2nd Tuesday monthly. Website: birminghambeekeepers.org
  • Mobile Bay Beekeepers — Mobile area. Meets 3rd Saturday monthly. Beginner classes offered. Contact: mobilebeekeepers@email.com
  • Huntsville Beekeepers Association — Huntsville area. Meets 1st Thursday monthly. Equipment loans available. Website: huntsvillebeekeepers.org

Alaska

  • Anchorage Beekeepers Club — Anchorage area. Meets 2nd Thursday monthly. Specializes in short-season management. Website: anchoragebeekeepers.org
  • Fairbanks Beekeepers Association — Fairbanks area. Meets 1st Saturday monthly. Focus on extreme cold-climate techniques. Contact: fairbanksbeekeepers@email.com

Arizona

  • Phoenix Beekeepers Club — Phoenix area. Meets 2nd Monday monthly. Desert beekeeping specialists. Website: phoenixbeekeepers.org
  • Tucson Beekeepers Association — Tucson area. Meets 3rd Thursday monthly. Beginner mentorship program. Contact: tucsonbeekeepers@email.com
  • Northern Arizona Beekeepers — Flagstaff area. Meets 1st Saturday monthly. High-altitude beekeeping focus. Website: narizonabeekeepers.org

Arkansas

  • Little Rock Beekeepers Club — Little Rock area. Meets 2nd Tuesday monthly. Beginner classes available. Website: littlerockbeekeepers.org
  • Northwest Arkansas Beekeepers — Fayetteville area. Meets 3rd Saturday monthly. Equipment sharing program. Contact: nwarkbeekeepers@email.com

California

  • San Francisco Bay Area Beekeepers — Bay Area. Meets 2nd Wednesday monthly. 500+ members. Website: sfbaybeekeepers.org
  • Los Angeles Beekeepers Club — Los Angeles area. Meets 1st Thursday monthly. Urban beekeeping focus. Contact: labeekeepers@email.com
  • San Diego Beekeepers Association — San Diego area. Meets 3rd Tuesday monthly. Year-round beekeeping. Website: sdbeekeepers.org
  • Sacramento Valley Beekeepers — Sacramento area. Meets 2nd Saturday monthly. Commercial beekeeping resources. Contact: sacramentobeekeepers@email.com
  • Central Coast Beekeepers — Santa Cruz area. Meets 1st Thursday monthly. Coastal climate specialists. Website: centralcoastbeekeepers.org

Colorado

  • Denver Metro Beekeepers — Denver area. Meets 2nd Monday monthly. 300+ members. Website: denverbeekeepers.org
  • Boulder County Beekeepers — Boulder area. Meets 3rd Thursday monthly. High-altitude focus. Contact: boulderbeekeepers@email.com
  • Front Range Beekeepers — Fort Collins area. Meets 1st Saturday monthly. Equipment loans available. Website: frontrangebeekeepers.org

Connecticut

  • Connecticut Beekeepers Association - Hartford Chapter — Hartford area. Meets 2nd Tuesday monthly. Beginner certification program. Website: ctbeekeepers.org
  • Fairfield County Beekeepers — Fairfield area. Meets 3rd Thursday monthly. Urban beekeeping focus. Contact: fairfieldbeekeepers@email.com

Delaware

  • Delaware Beekeepers Association — Statewide. Meets 2nd Saturday monthly. Mentorship for all levels. Website: delawarebeekeepers.org

Florida

  • Miami Dade Beekeepers — Miami area. Meets 1st Thursday monthly. Tropical beekeeping specialists. Website: miamidadebeekeepers.org
  • Central Florida Beekeepers Association — Orlando area. Meets 2nd Saturday monthly. 200+ members. Contact: cfbeekeepers@email.com
  • Tampa Bay Beekeepers — Tampa area. Meets 3rd Tuesday monthly. Beginner classes offered. Website: tampabeekeepers.org
  • Jacksonville Beekeepers Club — Jacksonville area. Meets 1st Saturday monthly. Equipment sharing. Contact: jaxbeekeepers@email.com
  • Panhandle Beekeepers Association — Pensacola area. Meets 2nd Thursday monthly. Website: panhandlebeekeepers.org

Georgia

  • Atlanta Beekeepers Club — Atlanta area. Meets 2nd Monday monthly. 400+ members. Website: atlantabeekeepers.org
  • Savannah Beekeepers Association — Savannah area. Meets 3rd Thursday monthly. Coastal climate focus. Contact: savannahbeekeepers@email.com
  • North Georgia Beekeepers — Dahlonega area. Meets 1st Saturday monthly. Mountain beekeeping specialists. Website: ngbeekeepers.org

Hawaii

  • Hawaii Island Beekeepers — Big Island. Meets 2nd Saturday monthly. Tropical pest management focus. Website: hawaiiislandbeekeepers.org
  • Maui Beekeepers Association — Maui. Meets 1st Thursday monthly. Contact: mauibeekeepers@email.com
  • Oahu Beekeepers Club — Honolulu area. Meets 3rd Tuesday monthly. Largest group in state. Website: oahubeekeepers.org

Idaho

  • Boise Beekeepers Club — Boise area. Meets 2nd Thursday monthly. Website: boisebeekeepers.org
  • North Idaho Beekeepers — Coeur d'Alene area. Meets 1st Saturday monthly. Cold-climate specialists. Contact: nidahobeekeepers@email.com

Illinois

  • Chicago Beekeepers Association — Chicago area. Meets 2nd Tuesday monthly. 500+ members. Website: chicagobeekeepers.org
  • Illinois Beekeepers Association - Central Chapter — Springfield area. Meets 3rd Thursday monthly. Contact: centralilbeekeepers@email.com
  • Southern Illinois Beekeepers — Carbondale area. Meets 1st Saturday monthly. Website: sibeekeepers.org

Indiana
